mechanism_narrative: A sustained decline in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) indicates a contracting economy and weakening aggregate demand, leading to lower corporate earnings expectations and negative equity returns over the medium term.
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS has experienced unprecedented declines since late 2008, reflecting the severity of the recession.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of instances where the 6-month change in PAYEMS is negative.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds Rate is at the zero lower bound, it signals that conventional monetary policy is exhausted and the economy is in a severe downturn. This condition depresses investor sentiment and expectations for future growth, leading to poor equity performance.
evidence_refs: fact:The FEDFUNDS rate is currently 0.16%, effectively at the zero lower bound since late 2008.
failure_condition: The average 60-day S&P 500 forward return is not statistically significantly negative during periods when FEDFUNDS is below 0.25% for at least 3 consecutive months.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous contraction in housing starts (HOUST) and retail sales (RSAFS) indicates a broad-based collapse in consumer and investment demand. This interaction has a stronger negative impact on expected corporate revenues and equity prices than either series in isolation.
evidence_refs: fact:The housing market collapse preceded the broader 2008 downturn, which was confirmed by falling retail sales.
failure_condition: A model including an interaction term for HOUST and RSAFS momentum does not show a statistically significant improvement in explanatory power over a model with only additive linear terms.

mechanism_narrative: In a post-recession recovery, a rebound in industrial production (INDPRO) from a deep trough signals that the supply side of the economy is healing and production is ramping up to meet nascent demand. This leads to improved earnings outlooks and positive equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO bottomed in June 2009 and has shown positive growth for two consecutive months, suggesting a recovery is underway.
failure_condition: The information coefficient of INDPRO 3-month change on S&P 500 20-day forward returns is not positive and significant in the 24 months following an NBER-defined recession trough.

mechanism_narrative: An unemployment rate (UNRATE) rising above a high threshold signals severe labor market slack, which suppresses wage growth and consumer spending. This has a direct negative impact on corporate profits and leads to negative equity performance.
evidence_refs: fact:The unemployment rate is currently 9.7%, a multi-decade high, indicating extreme economic distress.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 has a positive average 60-day forward return across all historical instances where the unemployment rate was sustained above 9.0% for 3+ months.

mechanism_narrative: Rising inflation (CPIAUCSL) while the central bank is constrained at the zero lower bound (low FEDFUNDS) creates a negative real interest rate environment. This erodes the value of future earnings and signals policy impotence, negatively impacting equity valuations.
failure_condition: In periods meeting the trigger condition, S&P 500 20-day forward returns are not statistically lower than in periods where FEDFUNDS is low but inflation is also low.

mechanism_narrative: An acceleration in real GDP growth (GDPC1) is the broadest indicator of improving aggregate demand. This translates directly to higher expected corporate earnings growth across the market, driving positive equity returns over a quarterly horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:After five quarters of contraction, real GDP is expected to show positive growth for Q3 2009, signaling a potential economic turning point.
failure_condition: The rolling 5-year correlation between quarter-over-quarter real GDP growth and subsequent 60-day S&P 500 returns is not consistently positive.

mechanism_narrative: In a post-recessionary environment with highly accommodative monetary policy, a deceleration in the rate of job losses (an improvement in the second derivative of PAYEMS) is a powerful leading indicator of a labor market bottom, sparking a strong positive equity market reaction over short horizons.
candidate_trigger: 1-month change in PAYEMS is greater than 3-month average change
evidence_refs: fact:The rate of job losses has slowed significantly from over -700k per month in early 2009.
failure_condition: When FEDFUNDS < 1%, periods where the rate of job losses decelerates are not followed by positive 5-day S&P 500 returns in more than 60% of instances.
